cards.front: What are the 3 RFC 1918 PRIVATE address ranges?

cards.back: 10.0.0.5 → Class A (private). 172.16.0.5 → Class B (private). 192.168.0.5 → Class C (private). 224.0.0.5 → Class D (multicast — used by OSPF AllSPFRouters). 169.254.0.5 → Class B but special (APIPA — link-local, when DHCP fails).

cards.front: What is APIPA, and what address range does it use?
cards.back: Automatic Private IP Addressing — 169.254.0.0/16. A host that fails to get a DHCP lease self-assigns an address from this range so it can talk to other APIPA hosts on the same L2 segment. Cannot route off-segment. Microsoft term; Linux uses Avahi/zeroconf.

cards.front: Why don't modern networks use classful routing protocols (RIPv1, IGRP) anymore?
cards.back: They don't carry subnet mask in routing updates, so they assume the classful default. This breaks VLSM and discontiguous subnets. CIDR (1993) and classless protocols (OSPF, EIGRP, RIPv2, BGP) carry masks in updates and route by longest-match instead.
